name.text: Week 13 – Q5: Bounce Diagram Transient Analysis

questiontext.text:
Bounce Diagram — Transient Analysis

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 7 points (6 parts: a–e worth 1 pt each, f worth 2 pts) | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A lossless transmission line has characteristic impedance \(Z_0 = {@Z0@}\;\Omega\), length \(\ell = {@l_m@}\;\text{m}\), and phase velocity \(v_p = {@vp_disp@} \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}\).

A step-function voltage source \(V_g = {@Vg@}\;\text{V}\) with series resistance \(R_g = {@Rg@}\;\Omega\) drives the line. The load is a resistor \(R_L = {@RL@}\;\Omega\). At \(t = 0\) the switch closes.

Part (a) — Load reflection coefficient (1 pt)

Compute the load reflection coefficient:

\[\Gamma_L = \frac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0}\]

\(\Gamma_L = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.333 or -0.5

Part (b) — Source reflection coefficient (1 pt)

Compute the source (generator) reflection coefficient:

\[\Gamma_g = \frac{R_g - Z_0}{R_g + Z_0}\]

\(\Gamma_g = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.333 or -0.5

Part (c) — Initial forward voltage wave (1 pt)

Compute the amplitude of the initial forward-travelling voltage wave:

\[V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot \frac{Z_0}{R_g + Z_0}\]

\(V_1^{+} = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] V

Syntax hint: Enter a number in Volts, e.g. 6.667

Part (d) — One-way travel time (1 pt)

Compute the one-way travel time of the line:

\[T = \frac{\ell}{v_p}\]

\(T = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] s

Syntax hint: Enter in seconds using scientific notation, e.g. 5e-9

Part (e) — Steady-state voltage at the load (1 pt)

Compute the DC steady-state voltage across the load (after all reflections have died out):

\[V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_g + R_L}\]

\(V_{\text{ss}} = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]] V

Syntax hint: Enter a number in Volts, e.g. 8.0

Part (f) — Voltage waveform at the load (2 pts)

Using the bounce diagram, determine the voltage \(V(z = \ell,\, t)\) at the load as a function of time. Place exactly 4 points on the interactive graph below at times \(t = T,\; 3T,\; 5T,\; 7T\), each at the correct voltage level for that time interval.

[[jsxgraph input-ref-ans6="ans6Ref" width="680px" height="440px"]] /* ── Configuration ── */ var paramsEl = document.querySelector('.q5-graph-data'); var yMax = parseFloat(paramsEl.getAttribute('data-ymax')); var yMin = parseFloat(paramsEl.getAttribute('data-ymin')); var Vss = parseFloat(paramsEl.getAttribute('data-vss')); var MAX_POINTS = 4; var SNAP = 0.5; /* ── Board setup ── */ var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(divid, { boundingbox: [-0.5, yMax, 8.5, yMin], axis: true, grid: true, showNavigation: true, showCopyright: false }); /* Axis labels */ board.create('text', [8, yMin + (yMax - yMin) * 0.06, 't / T'], { anchorX: 'right', fontSize: 14, fixed: true }); board.create('text', [-0.35, yMax - 2, 'V (V)'], { anchorX: 'right', fontSize: 14, fixed: true }); /* ── Reference lines ── */ /* Dashed horizontal line at V_ss */ board.create('line', [[0, Vss], [1, Vss]], { strokeColor: '#999', strokeWidth: 1.5, dash: 3, straightFirst: true, straightLast: true, fixed: true, highlight: false }); board.create('text', [8.2, Vss, 'Vss'], { anchorX: 'left', fontSize: 12, fixed: true, cssStyle: 'color:#888;' }); /* Vertical dashed guides at t/T = 1, 3, 5, 7 */ [1, 3, 5, 7].forEach(function (tx) { board.create('line', [[tx, 0], [tx, 1]], { strokeColor: '#ccc', strokeWidth: 1, dash: 2, straightFirst: true, straightLast: true, fixed: true, highlight: false }); }); /* ── Point management ── */ var pts = []; var stateInput = document.getElementById(ans6Ref); function snapVal(v) { return Math.round(v / SNAP) * SNAP; } function serializePoints() { if (pts.length === 0) { stateInput.value = '[]'; } else { var parts = []; /* Sort by x (time) before serializing */ var sorted = pts.slice().sort(function (a, b) { return a.X() - b.X(); }); for (var i = 0; i < sorted.length; i++) { parts.push('[' + snapVal(sorted[i].X()) + ',' + snapVal(sorted[i].Y()) + ']'); } stateInput.value = '[' + parts.join(',') + ']'; } stateInput.dispatchEvent(new Event('change')); } function addPoint(x, y) { if (pts.length >= MAX_POINTS) return null; var snx = snapVal(x); var sny = snapVal(y); var p = board.create('point', [snx, sny], { name: '(' + snx.toFixed(1) + ', ' + sny.toFixed(1) + ')', size: 5, face: 'o', strokeColor: '#0055d4', fillColor: '#3388ff', snapToGrid: true }); p.on('drag', function () { p.setName('(' + snapVal(p.X()).toFixed(1) + ', ' + snapVal(p.Y()).toFixed(1) + ')'); board.update(); serializePoints(); }); pts.push(p); return p; } function getMouseCoords(e) { var cPos = board.getCoordsTopLeftCorner(e), absPos = JXG.getPosition(e), dx = absPos[0] - cPos[0], dy = absPos[1] - cPos[1]; return new JXG.Coords(JXG.COORDS_BY_SCREEN, [dx, dy], board); } /* ── Restore state from saved answer on page reload ── */ if (stateInput.value && stateInput.value !== '' && stateInput.value !== '[]') { var matches = stateInput.value.match(/\[(-?[\d.]+),\s*(-?[\d.]+)\]/g); if (matches) { for (var i = 0; i < matches.length && i < MAX_POINTS; i++) { var nums = matches[i].match(/(-?[\d.]+)/g); if (nums && nums.length >= 2) addPoint(parseFloat(nums[0]), parseFloat(nums[1])); } serializePoints(); board.update(); } } /* ── Click to place points ── */ board.on('down', function (e) { var canCreate = true, coords, el, x, y; if (e[JXG.touchProperty]) return; coords = getMouseCoords(e); /* Check if clicking on an existing point (drag, not create) */ for (el in board.objects) { if (JXG.isPoint(board.objects[el]) && board.objects[el].hasPoint(coords.scrCoords[1], coords.scrCoords[2])) { canCreate = false; break; } } if (!canCreate || pts.length >= MAX_POINTS) return; x = snapVal(coords.usrCoords[1]); y = snapVal(coords.usrCoords[2]); /* Ignore clicks outside the plot area */ if (x < 0 || x > 8 || y < yMin || y > yMax) return; addPoint(x, y); serializePoints(); }); /* ── Reset button ── */ board.create('button', [5.5, yMax - 1.5, 'Reset', function () { for (var i = pts.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) board.removeObject(pts[i]); pts = []; board.update(); serializePoints(); }]); [[/jsxgraph]]

Click on the graph to place 4 key points showing the voltage \(V(t)\) at the load. Place points at times \(t = T,\; 3T,\; 5T,\; 7T\) with the correct voltage level at each step. You can drag points to adjust. The dashed grey line shows the steady-state voltage \(V_{\text{ss}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\).

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Load Reflection Coefficient \(\Gamma_L\)

The load reflection coefficient is:

\[\Gamma_L = \frac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0} = \frac{{@RL@} - {@Z0@}}{{@RL@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta1@}\]

Step 2: Generator Reflection Coefficient \(\Gamma_g\)

The generator (source) reflection coefficient is:

\[\Gamma_g = \frac{R_g - Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = \frac{{@Rg@} - {@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta2@}\]

Step 3: Initial Forward Voltage Wave \(V_1^{+}\)

At \(t = 0\), the source drives the line through a voltage divider formed by \(R_g\) and \(Z_0\):

\[V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot \frac{Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = {@Vg@} \cdot \frac{{@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\]

Step 4: One-Way Travel Time \(T\)

The one-way travel time is simply the line length divided by the phase velocity:

\[T = \frac{\ell}{v_p} = \frac{{@l_m@}}{{@vp@}} = {@ta4@}\;\text{s} \approx {@T_ns@}\;\text{ns}\]

Step 5: Steady-State Voltage \(V_{\text{ss}}\)

After all reflections have died out, the transmission line behaves as a simple wire. The DC steady-state voltage across the load is a resistive divider:

\[V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_g + R_L} = {@Vg@} \cdot \frac{{@RL@}}{{@Rg@} + {@RL@}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\]

Step 6: Bounce Diagram — Voltage at the Load \(V(z = \ell,\, t)\)

At the load (\(z = \ell\)), the voltage changes at odd multiples of the one-way travel time: \(t = T, 3T, 5T, 7T, \ldots\)

The initial forward wave \(V_1^{+}\) arrives at the load at \(t = T\). At each subsequent bounce, the reflected and re-reflected waves accumulate. The voltage at the load after each bounce is:

  • At \(t = T\): The first wave arrives. \[V(T) = V_1^{+}(1 + \Gamma_L) = {@ta3@} \times (1 + {@ta1@}) = {@V_step1@}\;\text{V}\]
  • At \(t = 3T\): After one round trip (reflection at source, then back to load). \[V(3T) = V(T) + V_1^{+} \cdot \Gamma_L \Gamma_g (1 + \Gamma_L) = {@V_step2@}\;\text{V}\]
  • At \(t = 5T\): Second round-trip reflection. \[V(5T) = V(3T) + V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^2 (1 + \Gamma_L) = {@V_step3@}\;\text{V}\]
  • At \(t = 7T\): Third round-trip reflection. \[V(7T) = V(5T) + V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^3 (1 + \Gamma_L) = {@V_step4@}\;\text{V}\]

As the bounces continue, the voltage converges to \(V_{\text{ss}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\).

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)\(\Gamma_L\){@ta1@}
(b)\(\Gamma_g\){@ta2@}
(c)\(V_1^{+}\){@ta3@} V
(d)\(T\){@ta4@} s
(e)\(V_{\text{ss}}\){@ta5@} V
(f)V(t) at loadPoints at (T, {@V_step1@}), (3T, {@V_step2@}), (5T, {@V_step3@}), (7T, {@V_step4@})

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Γ_L

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Γ_g

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — V₁⁺

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Travel Time T

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — Steady-State V_ss

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (f) — V(t) at Load (JSXGraph)

[[feedback:prt6]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: Z0={@Z0@}Ω, Rg={@Rg@}Ω, RL={@RL@}Ω, Vg={@Vg@}V | Γ_L={@ta1@}, V_ss={@ta5@}

questiondescription.text: Bounce diagram transient analysis — compute reflection coefficients, initial forward wave, travel time, steady-state voltage, and plot the voltage waveform at the load using an interactive JSXGraph. Week 13 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The load reflection coefficient is \(\Gamma_L = \dfrac{R_L - Z_0}{R_L + Z_0} = \dfrac{{@RL@} - {@Z0@}}{{@RL@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta1@}\).
Incorrect. The generator reflection coefficient is \(\Gamma_g = \dfrac{R_g - Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = \dfrac{{@Rg@} - {@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta2@}\).
Incorrect. The initial forward wave is found from the voltage divider at the source: \(V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot \dfrac{Z_0}{R_g + Z_0} = {@Vg@} \cdot \dfrac{{@Z0@}}{{@Rg@} + {@Z0@}} = {@ta3@}\;\text{V}\).

Incorrect. In the DC steady state, the transmission line acts as a simple wire. The voltage across the load is a resistive divider: \(V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_g + R_L} = {@Vg@} \cdot \dfrac{{@RL@}}{{@Rg@} + {@RL@}} = {@ta5@}\;\text{V}\).

node.description: AlgEquiv check — need at least 3 of 4 points correct

truefeedback.text:
Well done! Your V(t) plot is correct.

{@feedback_msg@}

falsefeedback.text:
Not quite right. Check the voltage levels at each bounce.

{@feedback_msg@}

hint.text: Hint 1 (Concept): When a step-function voltage source drives a transmission line through a series resistance, the initial voltage wave is determined by the voltage divider between \(R_g\) and \(Z_0\). Each time the wave reaches a discontinuity (load or source), it partially reflects. The reflection coefficients \(\Gamma_L\) and \(\Gamma_g\) control the amplitude of each reflected wave. Over time, the superposition of all waves converges to the DC steady-state value.

Hint 2 (Formulas): The key formulas are:

  • \(\Gamma_L = (R_L - Z_0)/(R_L + Z_0)\) — can be positive or negative
  • \(\Gamma_g = (R_g - Z_0)/(R_g + Z_0)\)
  • \(V_1^{+} = V_g \cdot Z_0 / (R_g + Z_0)\) — voltage divider at source
  • \(T = \ell / v_p\) — one-way travel time
  • \(V_{\text{ss}} = V_g \cdot R_L / (R_g + R_L)\) — DC voltage divider (line is transparent)

Hint 3 (Bounce diagram steps): At the load (\(z = \ell\)), voltage changes at \(t = T, 3T, 5T, 7T, \ldots\):

  1. At \(t = T\): \(V = V_1^{+}(1 + \Gamma_L)\)
  2. At \(t = 3T\): Add the contribution \(V_1^{+} \cdot \Gamma_L \Gamma_g (1 + \Gamma_L)\)
  3. At \(t = 5T\): Add \(V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^2 (1 + \Gamma_L)\)
  4. At \(t = 7T\): Add \(V_1^{+} \cdot (\Gamma_L \Gamma_g)^3 (1 + \Gamma_L)\)

Each successive bounce adds a factor of \(\Gamma_L \Gamma_g\) (which has magnitude < 1), so the steps get progressively smaller and converge to \(V_{\text{ss}}\).
